perturbation.original_text: Personal Data includes, without limitation, (i) an individual’s social security number, bank or other financial account numbers, credit or debit card numbers, driver’s license number, passport number, o ther government -issued identification numbers, biometric data, health and medical information, and data about the individual obtained through a Project; (ii) financial information, employee benefits information, education records, NU identification numbers
perturbation.changed_text: Personal Data includes, without limitation, (i) an individual’s social security number, bank or other financial account numbers, credit or debit card numbers, driver’s license number, passport number, o ther government -issued identification numbers, biometric data, health and medical information, and data about the individual obtained through a Project; (ii) financial information, employee benefits information, education records, NU identification numbers; (iii) publicly available directory information, such as name, address, and telephone number.
perturbation.explanation: By including publicly available directory information in the definition of 'Personal Data', the contract broadens the scope beyond what is typically protected under privacy laws, creating conflict and potential overreach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)
perturbation.law_explanation: FERPA does not consider directory information as protected if the educational institution has given public notice of the categories of information it has designated as such and allows students or parents to opt out of its release. This modification could create ambiguity around the handling of directory information, as the NDA now treats it with the same level of protection as highly sensitive personal data.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract includes 'publicly available directory information' within the definition of 'Personal Data,' which is problematic under FERPA. The Law Explanation states that FERPA doesn't protect directory information if the educational institution provides public notice and allows opt-outs. By treating directory information as equivalent to highly sensitive data, the modified NDA creates ambiguity and potential conflict with FERPA's provisions for handling such information.

perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information ” means all the information of the Disclosing Party furnish ed or made available , orally, in writing, by electronic means, or by inspection, at any time on and after the Effective Date regarding the Disclosing Party or its business plans , processes, technology, data, material, documents regarding the infrastructure, businesses, assets, liabilities, financial conditions, operations, identity of personnel, inte llectual property (including, without limitation, trade secrets), Personal Data (defined below ) and other related or its similar information provided in all cases such information is marked conspicuously with a confidentiality or a substantially similar le gend in accordance with Section 4 of this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information ” means all the information of the Disclosing Party furnish ed or made available , orally, in writing, by electronic means, or by inspection, at any time on and after the Effective Date regarding the Disclosing Party or its business plans , processes, technology, data, material, documents regarding the infrastructure, businesses, assets, liabilities, financial conditions, operations, identity of personnel, inte llectual property (including, without limitation, trade secrets), Personal Data (defined below ) and other related or its similar information. Marking such information is encouraged but not required.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for conspicuous marking of confidential information directly contradicts standard NDA practices and legal expectations for protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: While UTSA doesn't explicitly require marking, reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy are a key element in establishing trade secret status. Eliminating the marking requirement weakens the claim that the information was subject to reasonable secrecy efforts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha
perturbation.justification: The change removes the requirement for conspicuous marking of confidential information, which undermines efforts to maintain secrecy, a key aspect of trade secret protection. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that eliminating the marking requirement weakens the claim that the information was subject to reasonable secrecy efforts. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' and mentions a 'duty to limit acquisition, disclosure, or use' of trade secrets, implying a responsibility to actively protect such information, which the removal of the marking requirement contradicts by reducing a clear method of demonstrating the intent to keep information confidential.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall implement and maintain appropriate security measures for Personal Data which shall be at least as protective of the confidentiality of such information as the safeguards for personal information set forth under applicable federal, state and local law .
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall implement and maintain security measures for Personal Data that it deems commercially reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the standard of 'at least as protective as applicable law' with 'commercially reasonable' significantly lowers the bar for data protection and potentially conflicts with legal requirements for safeguarding personal information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law mandates specific requirements for protecting personal information. The 'commercially reasonable' standard could fall short of these requirements, creating a violation of the law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the requirement that data protection be 'at least as protective as applicable law' with a 'commercially reasonable' standard. This creates a legal contradiction because the 'commercially reasonable' standard might not meet the specific requirements for safeguarding personal information mandated by Massachusetts law, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93H, indicates that there are 'Regulations to safeguard personal information of commonwealth residents'. Replacing a standard based on applicable law with 'commercially reasonable' weakens the legally mandated protections, risking non-compliance with Chapter 93H.

perturbation.original_text: Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Recipient shall cease use of all Confidential Information furnished hereunder and shall, upon written direction of the Disclosing Party , return to the Disclosing Party , or destroy, all such Confidential Information, together with all copies made thereof by the Recipient . Upon request, the Recipient shall send the Disclosing Party a certificate con firming the destruction or return of all Confidential Information delivered hereunder. Notwithstanding the other provisions of this Section , Recipient may retain one copy of such Confidential Information for archival purposes only and such Confidential Information shall remain subject to this Agreement in accordance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement ,.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Recipient shall cease use of all Confidential Information furnished hereunder; however, the Recipient is not obligated to return or destroy such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon termination of the agreement directly contradicts the purpose of an NDA and creates a significant risk of continued misuse of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly a violation, retaining trade secrets after termination of the agreement and without authorization could lead to potential violations of the Economic Espionage Act, especially if the information is used to benefit a foreign entity or cause harm to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon termination, which undermines the fundamental purpose of an NDA. Although the scraped law snippet discusses the Economic Espionage Act, the Perturbation Explanation clearly states the modification 'directly contradicts the purpose of an NDA and creates a significant risk of continued misuse of the information.' The original text mandates the return or destruction of confidential information, whereas the changed text eliminates this requirement altogether, leading to a clear contradiction of the intended legal protection offered by an NDA. This falls under the definition of misaligned terminology creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The validity, interpretation, and effect of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts. The Parties hereby e xpressly consent to the exclusive personal jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts for any dispute arising from or relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity, interpretation, and effect of this Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts, except for disputes regarding intellectual property, which shall be governed by the laws of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing conflicting choice of law provisions for specific types of disputes creates ambiguity and could lead to forum shopping or jurisdictional issues.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose the governing law, introducing exceptions can create confusion, especially if the laws of Massachusetts and Delaware conflict on intellectual property matters. The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws emphasizes the importance of a clear connection to the chosen jurisdiction, which this modification weakens.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by creating conflicting choice of law provisions, specifying Massachusetts law for most disputes but Delaware law for intellectual property disputes. This contradicts the principle of clarity and consistency in contract law. The 'conflict of laws' definition from Wex states it refers to 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' By specifying different laws for different types of disputes arising from the same agreement, the changed text creates a potential conflict of laws scenario, making it unclear which jurisdiction's law should govern in specific situations, and creates potential ambiguity, which is the essence of legal contradiction in the definitions.
